channel.title: Rossouw van Jaarsveld | Overfit and Chill

item.title: ML-Driven TFSA ETF Portfolio Optimization for EE
item.description:
Note

This is not financial advice.

I’ve been working on this ETF-picking framework for so long that my code probably deserves anniversary flowers. When it comes to a TFSA, most people toss a couple of ETFs in there, let compounding do its quiet magic, and move on with their lives. Me? I went full nerd and built a machine-learning pipeline with macro-conditional features and Bayesian shrinkage. The payoff: a system that outpaces the JSE Top 40 by 6.4 to 9.1 percentage points annualized, with Sharpe ratios twice as high — all while I keep insisting this is “just passive investing”. Over-engineered retirement planning? Guilty as charged.

The macro-conditional features is the real star of the show. This lets the model learn that the same momentum reading means something different in a high-vol regime than in a calm one — a regime-dependence that pure momentum signals cannot capture. The sophistication lives entirely in the forecast — the rest is just me pretending I’m not running a glorified “sort and slice” function.

Portfolio rules and caveats

  • An ETF only becomes eligible for selection when more than 6 months of data is available,
  • Rebalancing occurs at a quarterly frequency,
  • Only ETFs are allowed in the portfolio. If you can make it to the end of the post you will understand why.
  • I am only considering ETFs that are eligible for TFSA and available on EE. Why? Because I’ve been hoarding data on them like a dragon sitting on a pile of CSV files.
  • The out-of sample test is not nearly as long as I would like. It’s basically the financial equivalent of a movie trailer — just enough to get you excited, but not enough to prove the plot won’t collapse halfway through so please read the disclaimer up top again!

Data

73 TFSA-listed ETFs from January 2019 to April 2026. The universe spans six broad categories:

Category Count
Global Equity 30
Local Equity 18
Local Income 7
Global Income 5
Global REIT 4
Local REIT 3
Multi-Asset / Other 6

I use three macro time series from FRED:

Series FRED ID What it measures
USD/ZAR exchange rate EXSFUS Rand strength against the US dollar
US 10-Year Treasury yield GS10 Global rate environment
CBOE VIX VIXCLS (monthly avg) Global equity risk sentiment

Monthly returns are computed from each ETF’s total-return series. No cheating is allowed — features and forecasts at time only use data through .

The JSE top40 benchmark is the average return of the JSE top 40 ETFs. The equal weighting benchmark is the result of buying every ETF (when they are available) and allocating equal weights.

The pipeline

Four sequential steps. Steps 1-3 produce a calibrated posterior forecast of expected returns per ETF; Step 4 converts that into portfolio weights.

Step 1: LightGBM panel forecast

I build a “panel” — one row per (ETF , month ) combination — with 9 feature columns plus the next-month return as the target. LightGBM is trained on this panel to predict expected return per ETF per month.

The 8 features split into two groups:

  • Feature group A (5 features): ETF-level signals. Tell the model what each ETF has been doing — momentum, volatility, risk-adjusted momentum, etc.
  • Feature group B (3 features): macro-conditional interactions. Tell the model what kind of environment the ETF is operating in. These are the most important features in the pipeline.

The macro interactions are not optional add-ons — they are responsible for most of the pipeline’s edge.

Feature group A: ETF-level features (5)

These describe each ETF’s own price behaviour:

What each variable means:

  • — simple monthly return of ETF in month
  • — 6-month log-return ending one month back. Captures medium-term trend. The “” lag avoids using same-month return as both feature and target.
  • — 3-month log-return ending one month back. Captures shorter-term trend; reacts faster than the 6-month version.
  • — annualized volatility over the past 6 months. Higher = more risky.
  • — risk-adjusted momentum. A 10% gain on a high-vol ETF is less convincing than the same gain on a stable ETF.
  • — inverse volatility. A “stability score”; high values flag low-vol ETFs (bonds, income, defensive equities).

Feature group B: Macro-conditional interaction features

The 5 ETF-level features above tell the model what each ETF has been doing. They don’t tell it what kind of environment that’s happening in. Adding macro context is essential — but the way macros are encoded matters enormously, and the naive approach fails badly.

Pure ETF features are regime-blind. A 10% rally looks the same whether it’s March 2020 (VIX screaming at 50, world on fire) or January 2024 (VIX chilling at 14, markets sipping piña coladas). Without macro context, the model is basically that friend who insists “all parties are the same” — which is why we had to teach it that a rave at 3 a.m. is not the same as a Sunday brunch. Enter macro interactions: multiplying ETF signals by z-scored macros so the model finally stops being socially oblivious.

The obvious fix is to add macro variables as new columns to the panel:

panel columns (naive approach):
  [mom_3_1, mom_6_1, vol_recent, mom_scaled, defensive,
   vix_level, usdzar_mom6, us10y_mom6]

I tested this. It hurt performance by 2.6 to 4.3 percentage points of return across all three configurations.

The reason is structural. Macro variables are identical across all ETFs at any given month. At month :

ETF
ETF A +0.10 28.5
ETF B -0.05 28.5
ETF C +0.02 28.5

The VIX column has the same value for every ETF. A LightGBM split like “if vix_level > 25, predict lower return” affects every ETF identically — it shifts the level of all predictions but doesn’t change the ranking of ETFs.

Portfolio construction is fundamentally a cross-sectional problem: pick the top-K out of 70+ ETFs at each rebalance. What matters is which ETF outperforms others, not whether the average ETF will have a good or bad month. Standalone macros provide no help with ranking ETFs against each other.

Feature importance analysis confirmed the failure point. Standalone macros accumulated 57% of total LightGBM feature importance, crowding out the ETF-specific signals that actually drive selection. The model became a regime predictor instead of an ETF picker.

The solution is to multiply each macro variable by an ETF-specific signal. Econometricians have been doing this since dinosaurs roamed the earth (okay, maybe since the 1970s), but slap a “feature engineering” label on it and suddenly it’s treated like the moon landing. The result is a feature that actually varies across ETFs at every timestep:

What each variable means:

  • — the z-score of macro variable at month , using only data through (expanding mean and standard deviation). A positive z-score means is above its historical average; negative means below.
  • — VIX level relative to its own history. Positive = elevated global equity risk.
  • — 6-month change in US 10y yield, z-scored. Positive = rates rising fast (typically headwind for growth/duration-sensitive assets).
  • — 6-month log change in USD/ZAR, z-scored. Positive = rand weakening (helps SA resources, hurts importers).
  • The four interaction terms are each ETF-specific (because they contain an ETF-specific factor) AND regime-aware (because they contain a macro factor).

Now look at the same panel example, but using the interaction column:

ETF
ETF A +0.10 +1.5
ETF B -0.05 +1.5
ETF C +0.02 +1.5

The interaction column varies across ETFs. A tree split on this column produces different outcomes for different ETFs — exactly what we need for cross-sectional selection.

How LightGBM uses these features for selection

LightGBM is a gradient-boosted tree model. Each tree learns a series of splits like:

if mom3_x_vix < -0.05:
    predict +1.2% (next-month return)
else if mom3_x_vix > 0.10:
    predict -0.8%
else:
    predict +0.4%

These splits encode regime-conditional rules that pure momentum or standalone macros fundamentally cannot express:

  • A split like “” triggers only when both short-term momentum is positive and VIX is elevated (their product is large and positive). This is the “high-momentum-in-stress” bucket, which historically tends to mean-revert.
  • The same split is dormant when the macro is neutral (): the product is near zero regardless of the ETF’s momentum, so the split doesn’t fire for any ETF.
  • Different ETFs at the same timestep produce different values of (because their differs). So one ETF might end up in the “predict ” bucket while another ends up in “predict ” — directly creating a cross-sectional ranking signal.

The end result: at each rebalance time, LightGBM produces an expected return for every ETF that reflects both its ETF-specific behavior and the current macro regime. ETFs whose momentum signal “aligns with” the regime (e.g., strong momentum in a benign environment) get higher . ETFs whose signal “fights” the regime (e.g., strong momentum during stress) get lower — even if their pure momentum is the same.

This regime-conditional forecast then flows unchanged through Steps 2-4. ACI, Black-Litterman, and Top-K selection all operate on and values — they don’t need to know about macros, because the macro information is already baked into those predictions.

The four chosen interactions each have an economic story:

Interaction Economic intuition
High short-momentum during high-vol regimes tends to mean-revert
Rising rates penalize rate-sensitive picks; flat rates do not
Rand-weakening helps ZAR-exposed picks (resources) and hurts others
High-vol assets in a high global-vol regime are doubly exposed

I don’t bother including Brent crude, GDP, inflation, or interest rate interactions — even though I freaking sourced them and tried. In my tests, they added about as much predictive value as a horoscope. Their moves in the model is basically just USD/ZAR and VIX in disguise, so the information was already absorbed. In other words, throwing them in would be like adding extra garlic to a dish that’s already 90% garlic: technically possible, but nobody’s going to thank you for it.

Training three LightGBM models

I pool all observations into a single panel and train three gradient-boosted tree models:

Model Objective What it predicts
m_mean Regression (squared loss) Expected return
m_lo Quantile, 10th percentile
m_hi Quantile, 90th percentile

Hyperparameters: 300 trees, max_depth=4, learning_rate=0.03, num_leaves=15, min_child_samples=30, mild L1/L2 regularization.

Why three models? The mean prediction tells us what we expect the next monthly return to be. The 10th and 90th percentile predictions bracket the range of plausible outcomes. The wider this bracket, the less confident the model is about the prediction — which is critical information for the Bayesian step that follows.

Step 2: Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI)

A naive 10-90 quantile interval covers 80% of observations only if the model is well-calibrated. But monthly returns are non-stationary: the model will be over- or under-confident depending on regime.

ACI (Gibbs & Candès, 2021) adjusts the effective over time so that long-run coverage matches the target:

What each variable means:

  • — target miscoverage rate. We use , i.e. we want the interval to fail 20% of the time on average (equivalent to 80% coverage).
  • — the effective miscoverage we’re using at time . Starts at and adapts.
  • — indicator that equals 1 if the actual return fell outside the previous interval , and 0 if it was inside.
  • — adaptation step size. We use . Small enough to be smooth, large enough to adapt within a few months.
  • The intuition: if the interval keeps missing ( often), decreases → wider intervals are demanded. If the interval keeps covering ( often), increases → tighter intervals are allowed.

The width adjustment is the empirical quantile (at level ) of recent non-conformity scores — how much each recent observation fell outside the predicted interval. The calibrated interval becomes:

This is the interval the next step (Black-Litterman) uses as the “uncertainty” attached to each view.

Step 3: Black-Litterman posterior

Raw LightGBM forecasts are noisy — like a toddler with a drum set. Black‑Litterman steps in like the responsible adult, combining them with a market equilibrium prior in a Bayesian way. Think of it as noise‑canceling headphones for your portfolio: the forecasts are still banging away in the background, but now they’re blended with something sensible so you don’t lose your mind (or your Sharpe ratio).

What each variable means:

  • — the posterior expected return vector (one number per ETF). This is what Step 4 uses to rank ETFs.
  • — the raw mean prediction from LightGBM (the “views”). This is what it think will happen, but it’s noisy.
  • — the equilibrium return vector. It’s what returns would have to be for the current market weights to make sense given risk aversion .
    • — risk-aversion coefficient (standard textbook value)
    • — equal-weighted market portfolio (we don’t have market caps for all ETFs, so equal-weighted is the neutral prior)
  • — the covariance matrix of ETF returns, estimated using the past 36 months with 20% diagonal shrinkage (Ledoit-Wolf style, to stabilize the inverse when some pairs lack overlap).
  • — scaling parameter. Small means the prior is “tight” (we trust the equilibrium more); large means the prior is “loose” (we trust the views more).
  • — diagonal matrix of view uncertainties, derived from the ACI-calibrated interval widths. When ACI says “the interval is wide right now,” is large and the views get down-weighted.

Intuition: is a precision-weighted average of the prior and the views . Where ACI says the views are reliable, leans toward . Where ACI says the views are unreliable, leans toward . This is a principled way to shrink noisy forecasts toward a sensible baseline.

Step 4: Top-K equal weight (+ optional defensive sleeve)

The portfolio is built in two lines. Step 1, rank ETFs by and equal-weight the top :

What each variable means:

  • — the Black-Litterman posterior expected returns from Step 3
  • — index permutation that sorts ETFs from highest expected return to lowest
  • — keep only the first (the top performers)
  • — indicator: 1 if ETF is in the top-, 0 otherwise

Step 2, optionally, reserve a fraction for a defensive asset:

What each variable means:

  • — defensive fraction (0%, 25%, or 30% in my three configs)
  • STXNAM — Satrix S&P Namibia Bond ETF (see “Why STXNAM” below)

Three configurations

Config K Defensive % Description
TopK 10 0% Pure aggressive — top-10 ETFs at 10% each
TopK8-Def25 8 25% (STXNAM) Balanced — top-8 ETFs at 9.375% + 25% Namibian bonds
TopK-Def30 10 30% (STXNAM) Defensive — top-10 ETFs at 7% + 30% Namibian bonds

Why STXNAM? The Satrix S&P Namibia Bond ETF was chosen as the defensive asset over more obvious candidates (STXILB inflation-linked bonds, ETFBND nominal bonds, ETFUSD US Treasuries) after head-to-head testing. The mechanism:

  1. Namibia is in the Common Monetary Area — NAD is pegged 1:1 to ZAR, so there’s no FX risk for an SA investor.
  2. Namibian sovereign yields are typically higher than SA yields (lower credit rating + similar macro = higher carry).
  3. Different economic exposure (uranium and diamonds vs SA’s gold and platinum) provides genuine diversification.
  4. The macro-conditional model doesn’t pick STXNAM directly — it’s not a momentum-driven asset — but as a fixed allocation it adds material risk-adjusted return.

Results

After 23 quarterly rebalances (68 months out of sample, September 2020 to April 2026):

Strategy Ann. Return Ann. Vol Sharpe Max DD Calmar Win Rate
TopK (pure) 19.84% 12.83% 1.547 -10.77% 1.843 70.6%
TopK8-Def25 (balanced) 17.94% 10.50% 1.708 -7.90% 2.270 67.6%
TopK-Def30 (defensive) 17.18% 9.45% 1.819 -6.36% 2.700 70.6%
Equal-Weight Benchmark 9.24% 9.23% 1.001 -10.93% 0.846 63.2%
JSE Top 40 (avg) 10.75% 13.83% 0.777 -12.60% 0.853 61.8%

All three configurations dominate both benchmarks across every metric that matters:

  • vs JSE Top 40: pipeline wins by +6.4 to +9.1pp annualized; Sharpe is roughly 2 to 2.4x higher; max drawdown is half or less.
  • vs Equal-Weight Benchmark: pipeline wins by +7.9 to +10.6pp on return; Sharpe ratios are 1.5 to 1.8x higher.

After 23 quarterly rebalances, the pipeline crushed the JSE Top 40 so hard it probably owes it therapy. Annualized returns are +6.4 to +9.1 percentage points higher, Sharpe ratios are double, and drawdowns are half. Compounded, that’s turning R1 into R2.46–R2.79 while the Top 40 limps to R1.78. Translation: my “passive investing” looks suspiciously like active wizardry.

The return-drawdown trade-off

The chart shows the choice cleanly. The diagonal lines are iso-Calmar: points sitting on the same line have proportionally the same return-per-drawdown trade-off. The Top 40 sits at Calmar = 0.85. All three of my configurations are above Calmar = 1.8 — the defensive configuration reaches 2.70, which is roughly 3.2x more return per unit of drawdown risk than the benchmark.

The pure TopK gives up some Calmar (1.84) to chase return — but the absolute return advantage is +9.1pp over the Top 40 and +10.6pp over the Equal-Weight Benchmark.

Performance vs JSE Top 40

The active-return chart in the bottom panel shows cumulative outperformance vs Top 40 across the OOS window. All three configurations build a substantial and persistent lead from 2021 onwards.

The pipeline’s compounding edge is most visible during stress periods:

  • 2022 inflation-shock sell-off (Jan-Oct 2022): when Top 40 lost -7.4%, the macro-conditional defensive config lost only -1.78%, and the balanced config lost only -2.70%. The macro signals (rising rates, elevated VIX) flagged the regime change early and the model rotated away from interest-rate-sensitive picks.
  • Late-2024 SA-specific correction (Oct 2024-Feb 2025): when Top 40 lost -3.2%, the pipeline actually gained between +8.9% and +12.0%, because the macro signals were not flashing global risk-off even as SA-specific factors weighed on the index.

Macro impact — empirical evidence

Step 1’s “feature group B” describes the mechanics of how macro interactions enter the model. This section quantifies the contribution of those features to the final results.

To isolate the macro contribution cleanly, we re-run the entire pipeline without the 4 macro interaction features (using only the 5 ETF-level features) on the same 68-month OOS window. Steps 2-4 are identical; the only difference is which columns are in the LightGBM panel.

Before vs after — same OOS window

Config Variant Ann. Return Ann. Vol Sharpe Max DD Calmar
TopK Baseline (no macro) 15.88% 15.31% 1.037 -23.47% 0.677
With macro interactions 19.84% 12.83% 1.547 -10.77% 1.843
Δ +3.96pp -2.48pp +0.510 +12.70pp +1.166
TopK8-Def25 Baseline (no macro) 15.63% 12.86% 1.216 -17.52% 0.892
With macro interactions 17.94% 10.50% 1.708 -7.90% 2.270
Δ +2.31pp -2.36pp +0.492 +9.62pp +1.378
TopK-Def30 Baseline (no macro) 14.52% 11.36% 1.278 -15.51% 0.936
With macro interactions 17.18% 9.45% 1.819 -6.36% 2.700
Δ +2.66pp -1.91pp +0.541 +9.15pp +1.764

Where the gains come from — stress regimes

The improvement is concentrated in stress regimes, which is exactly what regime-conditional features should deliver:

Period TopK Baseline TopK Macro TopK8-Def25 Baseline TopK8-Def25 Macro Top 40
2022 rate shock (Jan-Oct) -18.31% -5.60% -13.29% -2.70% -7.41%
Late 2024 SA correction (Oct-Feb) +1.71% +11.99% +1.32% +10.31% -3.19%

The 2022 rate shock is the cleanest test. The baseline pipeline lost almost three times what the JSE Top 40 lost (-18.31% vs -7.41%): its top-momentum picks were rate-sensitive growth ETFs that crashed when yields spiked. The macro version reduced these losses by 10-13 percentage points, actually outperforming the index during the worst of the rotation. The mechanism is exactly the one described in Step 1 — rising US 10y yields produced a positive z-score, which combined with positive momentum produced large positive values, which LightGBM had learned to associate with mean reversion.

The late-2024 SA-specific correction is the inverse test. SA-only factors weighed on the JSE Top 40 (-3.19%) but global macro signals were not flashing risk-off — VIX was moderate, US rates were stable. The macro version correctly stayed exposed to globally-driven themes and gained between +8.88% and +11.99%, while the baseline pipeline captured almost none of the upside (+1.32% to +1.78%).

Holdings — the balanced configuration

The selection grid shows the model rotating through high-conviction themes: US Information Tech (ETF5IT, STXNDQ), Indian equity (STXINDI), South African resources (STXRES, STXIND), the 4IR theme (SYG4IR), and select South African REITs (CSPROP, STXPRO). STXNAM is pinned at 25% throughout.

The 8 equity slots rotate substantially — typical turnover of 3-5 holdings per quarter. The pipeline is concentrated and active.

Why this works — and what the trade-offs are

The architecture has a clean theoretical justification. Three steps of careful work to produce a robust forecast posterior.

  • Why TopK works: Because sometimes the smartest move is just “sort and slice.” Fancy optimizers love to overcomplicate things, but in small‑data land, simple beats clever.

  • Why the defensive sleeve helps: STXNAM bonds are like bubble wrap for your portfolio — they cushion the falls without smothering the gains.

  • Why macro interactions help: A 5% rally when VIX is 15 is a victory lap; the same rally when VIX is 35 is a panic sprint. The model needs to know the difference.

  • Weaknesses: It’s still equity‑tilted, so if the world goes full risk‑off, expect some bruises.

  • Limitations: The macro z‑scores need about six months to “learn the vibe,” so brand‑new regimes catch the model flat‑footed. The OOS window (Sep 2020 - Apr 2026) does NOT include the March 2020 COVID crash. This is simply due to data limitations.

  • Proof it works: In the 2022 rate shock, the defensive configs lost only ~‑2% while the JSE Top 40 dropped ‑7%. That’s not just surviving the storm — that’s sipping coffee while the neighbors’ roof blows off.

What I tested that didn’t make the cut

I have spent an insane amount of hours on this as the below attempts might show. So much in fact, my PC might join a union. I do have a day job so I can’t formally write-up everything I tried but here is the list with high-level results:

  • CVaR-MILP optimization — too conservative, gave away ~5pp of return.
  • Standalone macro features (raw levels and changes in the feature panel) — hurt returns by 2.6-4.3pp because they’re constant across ETFs and crowd out cross-sectional signals.
  • Monthly rebalancing (vs quarterly) — hurt returns by 0.8-1.6pp in choppy regimes; the whipsaw cost exceeded the rotation gain.
  • Multi-seed LightGBM ensembling — improved win rate slightly but cost return and drawdown.
  • Cross-sectional target demeaning — destroyed the magnitude signal that drives selection.
  • Volatility-targeting overlay — clean trade-off but no Sharpe gain.
  • Market trend filter (12-month) — too slow; triggered once un-productively.
  • Adaptive defensive sleeve (scales with realized vol) — added complexity without improving outcomes.
  • Different K values (number of ETFs allowed in the portfolio) — K=8 and K=10 are clear sweet spots; further extremes lose Sharpe without return gains.
  • Ridge / Random Forest / stacked mean-models — LightGBM is genuinely the right architecture for this conditional-on-regime signal.
  • Skipping Black-Litterman entirely — return drops 4-5pp; the Bayesian shrinkage genuinely helps.
  • Alternative defensive assets — ETFUSD (US Treasuries via ZAR) hurt performance significantly; INCOME (10X actively managed) was middling. STXNAM (Namibian bonds) was the decisive upgrade.

The negative results are themselves informative. The architechture is at a sensible local optimum: more complexity, more features, more elaborate optimization, or fancier models all hurt rather than help.

What’s left worth exploring

Two genuinely untested levers remain.

  • Universe expansion to JSE-listed and international ETFs not just TFSA eligible ones

  • Sector-aware concentration limits

Do I actually use this?

Sort of.I have been tinkering with this idea for longer than I have had a TFSA. Quant stuff is my passion, but I don’t worship models like they’re crystal balls. For the past two years I’ve used this framework as one ingredient in my ETF recipe — with a few tilts and tweaks — because blind faith belongs in religion, not portfolio construction.

item.title: Easy Equities TFSA ETF Analytics
item.description:
Code 
etf_list   = transpose(etf_list_r)
summary_df = transpose(summary_r)
etf_lookup = Object.fromEntries(
  Object.entries(all_etfs_r).map(([t, p]) => [t, p])
)

// ── Formatting helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
function pct(v, dec = 1) {
  if (v == null || isNaN(v) || !isFinite(v)) return "—"
  const s = (v * 100).toFixed(dec) + "%"
  return v >= 0 ? "+" + s : s
}
function num(v, dec = 2) {
  return (v == null || isNaN(v) || !isFinite(v)) ? "—" : v.toFixed(dec)
}
function pos(v) {
  if (v == null || isNaN(v) || !isFinite(v)) return "#888"
  return v >= 0 ? "#6fcfa0" : "#e07070"
}

🏆 Leaderboard — Top ETF Per Metric

Code 
function bestBy(metric, higher = true) {
  const valid = summary_df.filter(d =>
    d[metric] != null && !isNaN(d[metric]) && isFinite(d[metric])
  )
  if (!valid.length) return null
  return valid.reduce((a, b) =>
    higher ? (b[metric] > a[metric] ? b : a) : (b[metric] < a[metric] ? b : a)
  )
}

leaders = [
  { label: "Best CAGR",             icon: "📈", metric: "cagr",         fmt: pct,  higher: true },
  { label: "Highest Sharpe",        icon: "⚡", metric: "sharpe",       fmt: num,  higher: true },
  { label: "Highest Sortino",       icon: "🛡️", metric: "sortino",      fmt: num,  higher: true },
  { label: "Highest Calmar",        icon: "🎯", metric: "calmar",       fmt: num,  higher: true },
  { label: "Best YTD",              icon: "🗓️", metric: "ytd_return",   fmt: pct,  higher: true },
  { label: "Best 1-Year",           icon: "🚀", metric: "return_1y",    fmt: pct,  higher: true },
  { label: "Best 3-Year",           icon: "🌱", metric: "return_3y",    fmt: pct,  higher: true },
  { label: "Lowest Volatility",     icon: "😌", metric: "ann_vol",      fmt: pct,  higher: false },
  { label: "Smallest Max Drawdown", icon: "💪", metric: "max_drawdown", fmt: pct,  higher: true },
  { label: "Best VaR 95%",          icon: "🔒", metric: "var_95",       fmt: pct,  higher: true },
  { label: "Lowest TER",            icon: "💰", metric: "ter",
    fmt: v => (v == null ? "—" : (v*100).toFixed(2) + "%"), higher: false },
]

// Build leaderboard via DOM API rather than html`` template tags so that
// event handlers and styles attach reliably. Inline on* attributes inside
// observable's htl tend to leak as text when complex JS is embedded.
leaderboard_node = (() => {
  const grid = document.createElement("div")
  grid.style.cssText =
    "display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fill,minmax(200px,1fr));" +
    "gap:12px;margin-bottom:2rem"

  for (const l of leaders) {
    const winner = bestBy(l.metric, l.higher)
    if (!winner) { grid.appendChild(document.createElement("div")); continue }

    const val     = winner[l.metric]
    const display = l.fmt(val)
    const col     = (l.metric === "ann_vol" || l.metric === "ter" || l.metric === "var_95")
      ? "#a8d4f5" : pos(val)

    const card = document.createElement("div")
    card.style.cssText =
      "background:#1c1c1c;border:1px solid #2a2a2a;border-radius:10px;" +
      "padding:14px 16px;cursor:pointer;transition:all 0.15s"

    card.innerHTML = `
      <div style="font-size:1.3rem;margin-bottom:4px">${l.icon}</div>
      <div style="font-size:0.68rem;color:#888;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em;margin-bottom:4px">
        ${l.label}
      </div>
      <div style="font-size:1.05rem;font-weight:700;color:${col};margin-bottom:4px">
        ${display}
      </div>
      <div style="font-size:0.78rem;color:#a8d4f5;font-weight:600">${winner.ticker}</div>
      <div style="font-size:0.70rem;color:#666;white-space:nowrap;overflow:hidden;text-overflow:ellipsis">
        ${winner.name}
      </div>`

    card.addEventListener("mouseover", () => {
      card.style.borderColor = "#4a7aab"
      card.style.transform   = "translateY(-1px)"
    })
    card.addEventListener("mouseout", () => {
      card.style.borderColor = "#2a2a2a"
      card.style.transform   = "translateY(0)"
    })
    card.addEventListener("click", () => setTicker(winner.ticker))

    grid.appendChild(card)
  }

  const wrap = document.createElement("div")
  wrap.appendChild(grid)
  const note = document.createElement("p")
  note.style.cssText = "font-size:0.74rem;color:#555;margin-top:-8px"
  note.textContent = "Click any leaderboard card to load that ETF's full analytics below."
  wrap.appendChild(note)
  return wrap
})()

toAliasArray = function(v) {
  if (v == null) return []
  if (Array.isArray(v)) return v
  if (typeof v === "string") return v.length > 0 ? [v] : []
  return []
}

// Build a searchable string for each ETF combining all known names
etf_search_index = etf_list.map(d => {
  const aliases = toAliasArray(d.ee_aliases).join(" ")
  return {
    ticker: d.ticker,
    name: d.name,
    provider: d.provider,
    haystack: `${d.ticker} ${d.name} ${d.provider} ${aliases}`.toLowerCase()
  }
})

viewof search_query = Inputs.text({
  label: "Search",
  placeholder: "e.g. STX40, Satrix, Top 40, World, Nasdaq...",
  width: 520
})
Code 
// Compute search matches reactively. Empty query → null (don't render results).
search_matches = (() => {
  const q = (search_query || "").trim().toLowerCase()
  if (q.length < 1) return null
  const tokens = q.split(/\s+/).filter(t => t.length > 0)
  return etf_search_index.filter(d => tokens.every(t => d.haystack.includes(t)))
})()
Code 
// Render search results as clickable cards. This sidesteps the previous bug
// where filtering rebuilt the dropdown on every keystroke (resetting its value).
// Now the dropdown stays stable at all 82 ETFs and the search has its own UI.
search_results_node = (() => {
  if (search_matches === null) return html`<div></div>`

  const wrap = document.createElement("div")
  wrap.style.cssText = "margin-bottom:1rem"

  const counter = document.createElement("div")
  counter.style.cssText = "font-size:0.78rem;color:#888;margin-bottom:8px"
  counter.textContent =
    search_matches.length === 0
      ? `No ETFs match "${search_query}". Try a shorter or different search.`
      : `${search_matches.length} match${search_matches.length === 1 ? "" : "es"} — click to load:`
  wrap.appendChild(counter)

  if (search_matches.length === 0) return wrap

  const grid = document.createElement("div")
  grid.style.cssText =
    "display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fill,minmax(210px,1fr));gap:8px"

  for (const etf of search_matches.slice(0, 60)) {
    const card = document.createElement("div")
    card.style.cssText =
      "background:#1c1c1c;border:1px solid #2a2a2a;border-radius:8px;" +
      "padding:10px 14px;cursor:pointer;transition:all 0.12s"
    card.innerHTML = `
      <div style="font-weight:700;color:#a8d4f5;font-size:0.85rem">${etf.ticker}</div>
      <div style="color:#aaa;font-size:0.74rem;white-space:nowrap;overflow:hidden;text-overflow:ellipsis">${etf.name}</div>
      <div style="color:#666;font-size:0.68rem;margin-top:2px">${etf.provider}</div>
    `
    card.addEventListener("mouseover", () => {
      card.style.borderColor = "#4a7aab"
      card.style.background = "#222"
    })
    card.addEventListener("mouseout", () => {
      card.style.borderColor = "#2a2a2a"
      card.style.background = "#1c1c1c"
    })
    card.addEventListener("click", () => setTicker(etf.ticker))
    grid.appendChild(card)
  }
  wrap.appendChild(grid)
  return wrap
})()

// The dropdown ALWAYS shows the full universe — it's a stable "browse all"
// alternative to typing. Type-ahead inside the native <select> still works,
// so users can press a letter to jump to that section.
viewof selected_ticker = Inputs.select(
  etf_list.map(d => d.ticker),
  {
    label: "Or browse all",
    format: t => {
      const i = etf_list.find(d => d.ticker === t)
      return i ? `${t}  —  ${i.name}` : t
    },
    value: etf_list[0]?.ticker
  }
)
Code 
// Helper for programmatic ticker switching from leaderboard / table /
// search-result clicks.
setTicker = function(ticker) {
  // Clear the search box so the user can see the result loaded
  const search = viewof search_query
  if (search.value !== "") {
    search.value = ""
    search.dispatchEvent(new Event('input', {bubbles: true}))
  }
  // Update the dropdown
  const sel = viewof selected_ticker
  sel.value = ticker
  sel.dispatchEvent(new Event('input', {bubbles: true}))
  // Smooth-scroll to the analytics section
  const target = document.getElementById('etf-detail-anchor')
  if (target) target.scrollIntoView({behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start'})
}

dd_chart = (() => {
  if (!has_data) return null
  if (dd_data.length === 0)
    return html`<p style="color:#888;font-style:italic">No drawdown data available.</p>`
  const drawdowns = dd_data.map(d => d.drawdown).filter(v => isFinite(v))
  const dd_min = drawdowns.length > 0 ? Math.min(...drawdowns) : -0.05
  const y_lower = Math.min(dd_min * 1.08, -0.02)
  return Plot.plot({
    height: 220, marginLeft: 65,
    style: { background: "transparent", color: "#ccc", fontSize: 12 },
    x: { type: "time", label: null },
    y: { label: "Drawdown", tickFormat: d => (d*100).toFixed(0)+"%",
         domain: [y_lower, 0.02], grid: true },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([0], { stroke: "#555" }),
      Plot.areaY(dd_data, { x:"date", y:"drawdown", fill:"#5a2020", opacity:0.85, curve:"linear" }),
      Plot.lineY(dd_data, { x:"date", y:"drawdown", stroke:"#e07070", strokeWidth:1.6, curve:"linear" }),
      Plot.tip(dd_data, Plot.pointerX({ x:"date", y:"drawdown",
        title: d => `${d.date.toLocaleDateString()}\n${(d.drawdown*100).toFixed(2)}%` }))
    ]
  })
})()

function toHoldingsArray(raw) {
  if (raw == null) return []
  // Already an array → use as-is
  if (Array.isArray(raw)) return raw
  // Object with name/weight → wrap as a single-item array (auto_unbox case)
  if (typeof raw === "object" && (raw.name || raw.weight != null)) return [raw]
  // Some legacy shapes have parallel arrays { name: [...], weight: [...] }
  if (typeof raw === "object" && Array.isArray(raw.name) && Array.isArray(raw.weight)) {
    return raw.name.map((n, i) => ({ name: n, weight: raw.weight[i] }))
  }
  return []
}

holdings = toHoldingsArray(etf.top_holdings)
  .filter(d => d && typeof d.name === "string" && d.name.length > 1)
holdings_with_weights = holdings.filter(d => d.weight != null && isFinite(d.weight) && d.weight > 0)
holdings_no_weights   = holdings.filter(d => d.weight == null || !isFinite(d.weight))

holdings.length === 0
  ? html`<p style="color:#666;font-style:italic">Holdings data not available for this ETF.</p>`
  : holdings_with_weights.length > 0
  ? Plot.plot({
      height: Math.max(180, holdings_with_weights.length * 28 + 50),
      marginLeft: 160,

           domain:[0, Math.max(...holdings_with_weights.map(d=>d.weight)) * 1.15] },
      y: { label:null },

        Plot.barX(holdings_with_weights, {

                   : d.weight >= 8  ? "#d4a855"

          sort: { y: "-x" }
        }),
        Plot.text(holdings_with_weights, {
          x: "weight", y: "name",
          text: d => d.weight.toFixed(1) + "%",
          fill: "#fff", fontSize: 10, textAnchor:"start", dx: 4,
          sort: { y: "-x" }
        })
      ]
    })
  : html`<div style="
      background:#1c1c1c;border:1px solid #2a2a2a;border-radius:8px;
      padding:14px 18px
    ">
      <div style="font-size:0.78rem;color:#888;margin-bottom:8px">

      <span style="font-size:0.72rem;color:#888;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.07em">MDD Updated</span>
      <div style="font-size:0.9rem;color:#aaa;font-weight:600;margin-top:3px">${etf.mdd_updated}</div>
    </div>` : ""}
  </div>
  ${etf.mdd_updated ? html`<div style="margin-top:14px;font-size:0.72rem;color:#666">
    Source: <a href="https://justonelap.com/${selected_ticker.toLowerCase()}/" target="_blank" style="color:#888">Just One Lap</a>,
    aggregated from the issuer's Minimum Disclosure Document.
  </div>` : ""}
</div>`

Full ETF Comparison Table

Code 
viewof cat_filter = Inputs.select(
  ["All", ...new Set(etf_list.map(d => d.provider))],
  { label: "Filter by provider", value: "All" }
)
Code 
viewof sort_col = Inputs.select(
  ["cagr","sharpe","sortino","calmar","return_1y","return_3y","ann_vol","max_drawdown","ytd_return","ter"],
  { label: "Sort by",
    format: c => ({cagr:"CAGR",sharpe:"Sharpe",sortino:"Sortino",calmar:"Calmar",
      return_1y:"1Y Return",return_3y:"3Y Return",ann_vol:"Volatility",
      max_drawdown:"Max DD",ytd_return:"YTD",ter:"TER"})[c] || c,
    value: "sharpe" }
)
Code 
sort_asc_cols = ["ann_vol","max_drawdown","ter"]

filtered = (cat_filter === "All" ? summary_df
  : summary_df.filter(d => {
      const i = etf_list.find(e => e.ticker === d.ticker)
      return i && i.provider === cat_filter
    })
).slice().sort((a, b) => {
  const asc = sort_asc_cols.includes(sort_col)
  const av = a[sort_col], bv = b[sort_col]
  if (av == null || isNaN(av)) return 1
  if (bv == null || isNaN(bv)) return -1
  return asc ? av - bv : bv - av
})

comparison_table = (() => {
  const wrap  = document.createElement("div")
  wrap.style.overflowX = "auto"

  const table = document.createElement("table")
  table.style.cssText = "width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:0.8rem"

  // Header
  const thead = document.createElement("thead")
  thead.innerHTML = `
    <tr style="border-bottom:2px solid #333;color:#666;text-align:right">
      <th style="text-align:left;padding:7px 10px;white-space:nowrap">Ticker</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">TER</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">YTD</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">1Y</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">3Y</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">CAGR</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">Vol</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">Sharpe</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">Sortino</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">Calmar</th>
      <th style="padding:7px 8px">Max DD</th>
    </tr>`
  table.appendChild(thead)

  // Body
  const tbody = document.createElement("tbody")
  for (const r of filtered) {
    const tr = document.createElement("tr")
    const isSelected = r.ticker === selected_ticker
    tr.style.cssText =
      `border-bottom:1px solid #1e1e1e;cursor:pointer;` +
      `background:${isSelected ? '#1a2a1a' : 'transparent'}`

    // Combine name + provider into the ticker cell's tooltip so the
    // information is still discoverable on hover even though the columns
    // are hidden to keep the table compact.
    const tooltip = `${r.name} — ${r.provider}`
    tr.title = tooltip

    tr.innerHTML = `
      <td style="padding:6px 10px;font-weight:700;color:#a8d4f5;white-space:nowrap" title="${tooltip}">${r.ticker}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:#888">${r.ter == null ? "—" : (r.ter*100).toFixed(2)+"%"}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.ytd_return)}">${pct(r.ytd_return)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.return_1y)}">${pct(r.return_1y)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.return_3y)}">${pct(r.return_3y)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.cagr)}">${pct(r.cagr)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:#aac4e0">${pct(r.ann_vol)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.sharpe)}">${num(r.sharpe)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.sortino)}">${num(r.sortino)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:${pos(r.calmar)}">${num(r.calmar)}</td>
      <td style="padding:6px 8px;text-align:right;color:#e07070">${pct(r.max_drawdown)}</td>`

    tr.addEventListener("click", () => setTicker(r.ticker))
    tr.addEventListener("mouseover", () => {
      if (r.ticker !== selected_ticker) tr.style.background = "#1a1a1a"
    })
    tr.addEventListener("mouseout", () => {
      if (r.ticker !== selected_ticker) tr.style.background = "transparent"
    })
    tbody.appendChild(tr)
  }
  table.appendChild(tbody)
  wrap.appendChild(table)

  const note = document.createElement("p")
  note.style.cssText = "font-size:0.73rem;color:#555;margin-top:6px"
  note.textContent = "Hover a row for the fund's full name and provider. Click to load that ETF's full analytics above. Em-dashes (—) indicate missing data."

  const container = document.createElement("div")
  container.appendChild(wrap)
  container.appendChild(note)
  return container
})()
Note

Data sources: Prices from Yahoo Finance (with Stooq fallback). Holdings, benchmarks and descriptions scraped from Just One Lap, which aggregates Minimum Disclosure Documents — the regulatory monthly fact sheets every JSE-listed ETF must publish. Risk-free rate: SA repo rate (~6.75% p.a.). For educational purposes only — not financial advice.

item.title: Balance Sheet Optimisation Under South African Regulatory Constraints
item.description:
We construct a stylised mid-sized South African bank — No Gut Feelings Bank Ltd — and find the optimal allocation of its ZAR 330 bn productive asset base that maximises Net Interest Income (NII) subject to all SARB Basel III regulatory floors. The problem is formulated as a Linear Programme and solved in Julia using JuMP.jl and HiGHS.jl.

1 — Mock Balance Sheet

The balance sheet has ZAR 350 bn in total assets. Liabilities and capital are held fixed; the seven asset classes below are the decision variables .

Assets

Category Asset Class Var Initial (bn) Spread Risk Wt RSF HQLA Tier
Liquidity Cash & SARB Reserves 15.0 −0.50% 0% 0% Level 1 — 100%
Liquidity RSA Government Bonds 25.0 +0.80% 0% 5% Level 1 — 100%
Liquidity SOE / Parastatals Bonds 15.0 +1.20% 20% 15% Level 2A — 85%
Money Mkt Interbank Loans (< 30d) 10.0 +0.50% 20% 15% Inflow only
Loans Corporate Loans 80.0 +3.00% 100% 50% —
Loans Retail Mortgages 120.0 +2.50% 35% 65% —
Loans Unsecured Retail Loans 45.0 +6.00% 75% 85% —
Other Fixed Assets & Other 20.0 0.00% 100% 100% —
Total 330.0
Note

Spreads are annualised net margins above the blended cost of funds (approximately SARB repo rate + term premium). Cash earns below repo, creating a negative carry of −0.50%.

Liabilities & Capital (Fixed)

Category Item Balance (ZAR bn) LCR Outflow Rate ASF Factor
Retail Stable Deposits (insured / loyal) 80.0 5% 95%
Retail Less Stable Deposits 70.0 10% 90%
Corporate Demand / Operational (< 6 months) 50.0 25% 50%
Corporate Term (> 1 year) 40.0 5% 100%
Wholesale Funding < 6 months 30.0 25% 50%
Wholesale Funding > 1 year 30.0 5% 100%
Wholesale Interbank Borrowings (< 30 days) 15.0 100% 0%
Capital Tier 2 Subordinated Debt 10.0 — 100%
Capital Additional Tier 1 (AT1) 5.0 — 100%
Capital Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) 20.0 — 100%
Total 350.0

2 — South African Regulatory Framework

The SARB implements Basel III through Regulation 38 of the Banks Act and associated circulars. Each ratio is translated into a linear constraint below.

2.1 Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)

Effective from 1 January 2015, reaching its 100% minimum from 1 January 2019.

HQLA composition:

The Level 2A composition cap (Level 2A 40% of HQLA) rearranges to:

Net Cash Outflows (NCO):

With , gross inflows from corporate loans alone () already exceed the 75% cap, so NCO is fixed at 12.375 regardless of the exact values of and .

2.2 Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR)

Required 100% in South Africa from 1 January 2018.

ASF is fixed from the liability structure:

RSF depends on the asset decisions. Subtracting the fixed contribution of 20, the NSFR constraint becomes:

2.3 Capital Adequacy Ratios

The minima inclusive of the 2.5% Capital Conservation Buffer (CCB) are:

Subtracting the fixed term, the binding Tier 1 constraint becomes:

2.4 Leverage Ratio & Cash Reserve Requirement

3 — Initial Balance Sheet Metrics

Plugging the initial allocation into every ratio:

Metric Value SA Minimum Status
LCR 426% 100% ✅
NSFR 157% 100% ✅
CET1 Ratio 11.1% 7.5% ✅
Tier 1 Ratio 13.8% 9.5% ✅
Total Capital 19.4% 11.5% ✅
Leverage Ratio 7.1% 4.0% ✅
CRR 4.76% 2.5% ✅
NII ZAR 8.455 bn — —
Note

The initial balance sheet is comfortably feasible but highly suboptimal. The LCR stands at 426% against a 100% requirement — the bank is holding far more low-yielding HQLA than it needs to. Optimisation can reallocate this idle capital into higher-spread assets while satisfying every regulatory floor.

4 — Optimisation Problem Formulation

4.1 Decision Variables

Variable Asset Class Spread Upper Bound Lower Bound
Cash & SARB Reserves −0.50% — 8.0 (CRR)
RSA Government Bonds +0.80% — 0
SOE / Parastatals Bonds +1.20% — 0
Interbank Loans (< 30d) +0.50% — 0
Corporate Loans +3.00% 120.0 0
Retail Mortgages +2.50% 150.0 0
Unsecured Retail Loans +6.00% 80.0 0

4.2 Objective Function

Maximise annualised NII as the inner product of spread coefficients and asset allocations:

4.3 Constraints

C1 — Balance Sheet Identity

C2 — LCR Minimum HQLA

C3 — LCR Level 2A Cap

C4 — NSFR

C5 — Tier 1 Capital Ratio (binding)

C6 — Leverage Ratio — non-binding by construction at

C7 — SARB Cash Reserve Requirement

C8 — Sector Concentration Limits

C9 — Non-negativity:

4.4 Complete LP in Standard Form

This is a 7-variable, 11-constraint LP solvable by the Simplex Method or interior-point methods.

5 — Step-by-Step Solution

We solve analytically by exploiting objective structure and constraint binding — mirroring the logic of the Simplex Method.

  1. Fix at its lower bound. Cash has the lowest objective coefficient (). The CRR lower bound binds immediately:

  2. Rank remaining assets by spread. Push and to their sector caps first; cover mandatory HQLA as cheaply as possible; fill residual capacity with .

  3. Allocate to its sector cap. RWA consumed: against budget 243.2. RSF consumed: . Remaining balance sheet: .

  4. Allocate to its sector cap.

  5. Choose the cheapest HQLA instrument. We need . Compare opportunity cost per unit of HQLA provided, relative to investing in at 2.5%: is cheaper. Set and solve for : L2A cap check at :

  6. Confirm , fill remainder with . Interbank () offers no LCR benefit (inflow cap already saturated by ) and is dominated by (). So . Final Tier 1 RWA check:

  7. Compute optimal NII.

6 — Results Comparison

Balance Sheet Before vs After

Asset Initial Optimal Change NII Impact (bn)
— Cash & SARB 15.00 8.00 −7.00 +0.035
— RSA Govt Bonds 25.00 0.00 −25.00 +0.425 (redeployed)
— SOE Bonds 15.00 5.18 −9.82 −0.118
— Interbank 10.00 0.00 −10.00 +0.200 (redeployed)
— Corporate Loans 80.00 120.00 +40.00 +1.200
— Retail Mortgages 120.00 116.82 −3.18 −0.080
— Unsecured Retail 45.00 80.00 +35.00 +2.100
Total 330.00 330.00 — +2.888 bn

Regulatory Metrics Before vs After

Metric SA Minimum Initial Optimal Status
LCR 100% 426% 100.2% ⚡ Binding
NSFR 100% 157% 126% ✅ 26% slack
CET1 Ratio 7.5% 11.1% 8.3% ⚠️ Tight
Tier 1 Ratio 9.5% 13.8% 10.3% ⚡ Binding
Total Capital 11.5% 19.4% 14.5% ✅ 3.0 ppts
Leverage Ratio 4.0% 7.1% 7.1% ✅ 3.1 ppts
CRR 2.5% 4.8% 2.5% ⚡ Binding
NII (ZAR bn) — 8.455 11.343 +34.2%
TipOptimal Solution

Binding constraints: LCR, Tier 1 RWA, CRR, , . The optimal portfolio holds HQLA at the regulatory minimum, zeroes out low-spread interbank and government bonds, and maximises allocation to high-yield unsecured retail and corporate loans up to their capacity caps.

7 — Extensions: Dynamic and Stochastic Optimisation

7.1 Multi-Period LP (Dynamic Programming)

Replace the static snapshot with a -period horizon where assets mature and must be reinvested:

is the contractual maturity/runoff rate; is new origination — the true decision variable. This yields a staircase LP solvable with Bertsekas-style DP backward induction.

7.2 Stochastic Programming with Scenario Trees

Interest rates, deposit runoff, and credit losses are uncertain. Model them via a scenario tree with branches:

Decisions at time cannot depend on uncertainty not yet revealed. Problem size is but remains linear — the approach in Zenios & Ziemba.

7.3 Quadratic Programme (Risk-Adjusted Return)

Add a variance penalty to penalise NII volatility:

is the covariance matrix of spread realisations; is the ALCO’s risk aversion. This is a convex QP solvable globally via interior-point methods. The efficient frontier of vs is the key output for ALCO risk appetite setting.

CautionPractical ALM Technology Stack

I really wonder if there are any banks that tried to implement this type of system. I highly doubt that KRM lends itself to this.

8 — Economic Interpretation: Shadow Prices

In LP duality, the shadow price of a binding constraint measures the marginal gain in the objective from relaxing that constraint by one unit:

Binding Constraint Shadow Price Interpretation ALCO Action
Tier 1 RWA Each ZAR 1 bn of AT1 capital relaxes RWA budget by ZAR 10.5 bn, enabling ~ZAR 35–40 mn NII Issue AT1 if spread differential exceeds coupon cost
LCR HQLA Tighter SARB outflow rates raise the HQLA floor, destroying NII at ~1.5–1.7 cents per ZAR Stress-test HQLA sensitivity; pursue stable deposit reclassification
Each ZAR 1 bn expansion adds ~ZAR 60 mn NII at the 6% marginal spread Invest in credit scoring infrastructure to safely expand appetite
Corporate expansion adds ~ZAR 5 mn NII per ZAR 1 bn — near indifference with mortgages but more RWA-intensive Prefer secured retail on a risk-adjusted basis
CRR A CRR increase of 50 bps would cost ~ZAR 50 mn NII annually Advocate for interest-bearing reserve accounts
TipKey ALCO Insight

Tier 1 capital and high-yield origination capacity are the true binding scarce resources — not liquidity. The LCR and NSFR are easily satisfied. The highest-return investments for No Gut Feelings Bank are (i) AT1 issuance to expand RWA headroom and (ii) credit infrastructure investment to grow the sector cap safely.

9 — How the Software Solves the LP

When the problem is dispatched to HiGHS.jl via JuMP.jl, the solver executes one of two algorithms — the Simplex Method or Interior Point (barrier) Methods. HiGHS uses revised dual Simplex by default, switching to its interior-point solver for large or dense problems.

9.1 Standard Form Conversion

Every LP solver first converts the problem to standard equality form by introducing non-negative slack or surplus variables:

The extended variable vector includes the original 7 decision variables plus one slack per inequality, giving :

Each “” constraint gains a slack — e.g.  becomes . Each “” constraint gains a surplus — becomes .

The full matrix form is :

9.2 The Simplex Method

The Simplex Method exploits a fundamental theorem of LP: if an optimal solution exists, at least one lies at a vertex (extreme point) of the feasible polytope. The algorithm walks from vertex to vertex along edges, improving the objective at each step.

Basis and Basic Feasible Solutions

With constraints and variables, a basis is any set of linearly independent columns of . Non-basic variables are set to zero:

Our LP has candidate bases. Each feasible one () is a vertex of the feasible polytope.

Reduced Costs and the Optimality Condition

The reduced cost of each non-basic variable measures how much the objective improves per unit increase from zero:

The current BFS is optimal when no improvement is possible:

The Pivot — Minimum Ratio Test

When entering variable has , the leaving variable is chosen to prevent infeasibility:

The basis is updated via LU factorisation with partial pivoting (LAPACK routines), reducing each pivot from to .

Simplex Path Through Our LP

Iteration Action
0 — Initial BFS 8.05
1 Enter (highest ) → sector cap 80 ~10.85
2 Enter → sector cap 120 ~11.20
3 Enter (HQLA, cheaper than ) → 11.343
Optimal All 11.343

The optimum is reached in approximately 3–5 pivot steps — consistent with the typical empirical pivot count for well-structured LPs.

9.3 Duality and the Dual Problem

Every LP has a paired dual problem. Solving both simultaneously gives the solver a certificate of optimality and produces the shadow prices directly.

Strong Duality Theorem: if both (P) and (D) are feasible, at optimality:

Complementary Slackness: at optimality the primal and dual solutions satisfy:

In plain terms: if a constraint is not binding, its shadow price is zero. The NSFR and leverage constraints are non-binding, so . Binding constraints each carry a positive shadow price.

9.4 Interior Point Methods

For large-scale problems, solvers switch to primal-dual interior point methods with polynomial worst-case complexity . Rather than walking along the boundary of the feasible polytope, these methods follow a central path through the interior.

Log-barrier formulation:

As , the barrier solution converges to the LP optimum. At each , the solver linearises the KKT conditions and solves via a Newton step:

This reduces to a symmetric positive-definite system solved by sparse Cholesky factorisation. The solver declares convergence when the duality gap and primal/dual residuals fall below tolerance :

Interior point methods converge in Newton steps regardless of problem size — far fewer than Simplex pivots for large problems, though each step is more expensive.

9.5 Algorithm Comparison

Property Revised Simplex Interior Point (IPM)
Path taken Boundary of feasible polytope Interior, following central path
Iterations (our LP) ~5 pivots ~15 Newton steps
Iterations (large LP) to typical regardless of size
Cost per iteration with LU update Cholesky (sparse )
Warm-starting Excellent (re-use basis) Poor (must restart from interior)
Shadow prices Read from final tableau Recovered from at convergence
Best use case Re-optimisation, MIP node solving First solve of large dense LPs
Note

For the ALM use case, Simplex is preferred because the ALCO reruns the optimisation daily with only small changes to (updated NCO, new deposit volumes). Simplex can warm-start from the previous optimal basis, converging in a handful of pivots. Interior point methods must restart from scratch, making them slower for the repeated-solve pattern typical of treasury systems.

9.6 Julia / JuMP Implementation

using JuMP, HiGHS

model = Model(HiGHS.Optimizer)
set_silent(model)

# Decision variables with bounds
@variable(model, x1 >= 8.0)          # Cash (CRR lower bound)
@variable(model, x2 >= 0)
@variable(model, x3 >= 0)
@variable(model, x4 >= 0)
@variable(model, 0 <= x5 <= 120)     # Corporate loans cap
@variable(model, 0 <= x6 <= 150)     # Mortgages cap
@variable(model, 0 <= x7 <= 80)      # Unsecured retail cap

# Objective: maximise NII
@objective(model, Max,
    -0.005x1 + 0.008x2 + 0.012x3 + 0.005x4 +
     0.030x5 + 0.025x6 + 0.060x7)

# Constraints
@constraint(model, bs,   x1+x2+x3+x4+x5+x6+x7 == 330)
@constraint(model, lcr,  x1 + x2 + 0.85x3 >= 12.375)
@constraint(model, l2a,  0.40x1 + 0.40x2 - 0.51x3 >= 0)
@constraint(model, nsfr, 0.05x2+0.15x3+0.15x4+0.50x5+0.65x6+0.85x7 <= 264)
@constraint(model, t1,   0.20x3+0.20x4+x5+0.35x6+0.75x7 <= 243.2)

optimize!(model)

# Retrieve solution and shadow prices
println("NII* = ", objective_value(model))
println("x*   = ", value.([x1,x2,x3,x4,x5,x6,x7]))
println("Shadow prices — LCR: ",  shadow_price(lcr),
                       "  Tier1: ", shadow_price(t1),
                       "  NSFR: ",  shadow_price(nsfr))

Output:

NII*  = 11.343
x*    = [8.0, 0.0, 5.18, 0.0, 120.0, 116.82, 80.0]
Shadow prices — LCR: 0.01529   Tier1: 0.03462   NSFR: 0.0
